nativeRange.habitat: The Pinnatus Batfish is reef-associated throughout its range. Juveniles tend to shelter in mangroves, sheltered coastal reefs, caves, and under ledges; adults are generally solitary, found beneath reef overhangs at depths of about 15–30 m.

decorPreferences: tall aquascape (vertical clearance for tall-bodied fish)

diet.notes: In the wild, the Pinnatus Batfish has been documented eating algae, jellyfish, gelatinous zooplankton, and small invertebrates. In captivity it is notoriously difficult to feed and frequently refuses prepared foods, particularly as a juvenile. A varied diet of meaty foods — mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and chopped shrimp, scallops, or mussels — alongside marine algae and seaweed is generally advised, offered in small amounts multiple times daily. Live foods such as live brine shrimp and small live shrimp are commonly required to initiate feeding in newly imported wild specimens; tank-raised juveniles tend to adapt more readily to prepared foods.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by prilfish. ~1024px. Iconic juvenile phase: jet-black body with brilliant orange/crimson margins outlining body and fins, sharp, whole fish framed against dark background. The single most striking, diagnostic image.
Photo by desertnaturalist. 1852x1854. Sharp, well-lit sub-adult on a colourful reef showing the tall falcate dark-margined dorsal, pale body and yellow fins as the red margin fades with age.

Photo by Dan Schofield. 2048x1536. Transitional juvenile with clear orange fin margins on tall dorsal/anal fins; dive shot is dark and carries a light photographer name watermark, so kept only as a non-recommended phase example.

summary: The Pinnatus Batfish (Platax pinnatus) is a tall-bodied Western Pacific reef fish whose jet-black juvenile body is rimmed with brilliant crimson margins, fading to a duller silver-brown as the fish matures. It is widely regarded as an expert-only species due to its notorious refusal to feed in captivity and high stress sensitivity.
careNotes:
The Pinnatus Batfish is one of the most challenging marine fish regularly seen in the aquarium trade, and is widely regarded as an expert-only species. Newly imported wild-caught specimens frequently refuse to eat and waste away within weeks of arrival. Tank-raised juveniles, when available, tend to fare significantly better.

Tank size and footprint: An absolute minimum of around 757–1136 L (200–300 US gal) is generally recommended, with some sources suggesting 1200 L or more. Depth (front-to-back) and height matter as much as length; this very tall-bodied fish is commonly cramped in standard-profile reef tanks.

Juvenile/adult color phase: Juveniles display a dramatic jet-black body with brilliant crimson margins outlining the entire body, fins, and tail — a coloration thought to mimic toxic flatworms (Pseudoceros). The red margins fade with maturity, and adults transition to a dull silver-brown to silver-tan with vertical banding and shorter fins. The visual change is extreme enough that juveniles and adults are sometimes mistaken for different species.

Feeding: The most cited reason for keeper failure. Many wild-caught individuals never adapt to prepared foods. Live brine shrimp, small live ghost shrimp, and live black worms are commonly used to initiate feeding; gradual weaning onto frozen mysis, enriched brine, and chopped seafood is the typical path. Macroalgae and seaweed strips on a clip have been reported to be accepted by acclimated individuals. Feeding multiple small meals per day is generally advised.

Reef compatibility: This species is not considered reef safe. It has been documented grazing on coral polyps, anemones, and small ornamental invertebrates. A FOWLR (fish-only-with-live-rock) configuration is generally recommended.

Health and shipping: Highly susceptible to marine ich (Cryptocaryon) and marine velvet (Amyloodinium), particularly during and shortly after shipping. Long quarantine periods and slow acclimation are generally advised. Shipping mortality is reported to be high, which contributes to the species' reputation for poor captive survival.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the home-aquarium hobby. Like other Ephippidae, spawning in the wild is believed to be pelagic broadcast spawning, with larvae developing in open water before juveniles settle into mangroves and sheltered coastal reefs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care sheet: 200–300 gal tank, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, expert-only, peaceful but not reef safe, juveniles have red border that fades, adults reach ~20 in, omnivore requiring varied meaty foods three times daily.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Ephippidae, order Acanthuriformes — note LiveAquaria's 'Ephippididae' spelling is incorrect), max length 45 cm TL, depth range 15–30 m, temperature 25–29 °C, native range Western Pacific Ryukyu Islands to Australia (Indian Ocean occurrence doubtful), IUCN Least Concern, diet on algae/jellyfish/gelatinous zooplankton, adults solitary under reef overhangs, juveniles in mangroves.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Confirmed minimum 200 gal (ideally 300+), 77–80°F, pH 8.2–8.4, SG 1.018–1.025, lifespan 5–12 years (up to 14 recorded), peaceful temperament, omnivore, juvenile color phase, highly susceptible to ich and marine velvet, good tankmates list, fast growth.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 45 cm, recommended minimum tank 1200 L, ~26 °C, SG ~1.026, mostly peaceful but possible aggression toward conspecifics, not reef safe (threatens anemones, shrimps, crabs, corals), delicate, susceptible to marine ich when stressed.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented in the home aquarium hobby. Like other members of the family Ephippidae, it is believed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner, releasing eggs and sperm into open water, with larvae developing in the water column before juveniles eventually settle into mangroves and sheltered coastal reefs.
